one_line_takeaway: A three-part Harvard dissertation on trustworthy AI whose only AI-security-relevant contribution is a family of information-theoretically optimal, distortion-free LLM watermarks (CC, SimplexWater, HeavyWater) for content-provenance detection, achieving up to 250% higher detection strength than Red-Green watermarking at comparable text/code quality; its other two pillars (predictive-model arbitrariness and a Beer-Game multi-agent LLM supply-chain simulator showing up to 67% cost reduction vs. human teams) are reliability/responsible-ML work, not adversarial security.
key_finding: Watermarking: On the HumanEval coding benchmark (Table 4.1, pass@k, higher better), the unwatermarked baseline scores pass@1=14.0%, pass@5=20.7%, pass@10=28.1%; HeavyWater scores 13.1/18.9/27.8, SimplexWater scores 13.7/22.7/25.3, Gumbel scores 14.3/20.5/25.6, Inverse Transform scores 13.8/22.0/27.5, and Red/Green (delta=3) scores 11.6/19.5/23.2 -- i.e., HeavyWater and SimplexWater preserve functional code quality comparably to the unwatermarked baseline and to competing watermarks (Gumbel edges out on pass@1; SimplexWater is best on pass@5), rather than uniformly dominating. On detection-distortion tradeoff, swapping the Red-Green (delta=2) cost function for SimplexWater/HeavyWater while holding the hashing scheme fixed yields up to a 250% improvement in aggregated -log10(p) detection strength (Fig. 4.10b), and both new schemes require fewer generated tokens to reach a fixed detection strength (p=1e-3) at zero distortion (Fig. 4.11). Multi-agent supply chain: the best configuration (Llama 4 Maverick 17B with optimized prompts, curated data sharing, and guardrails) reduced total supply-chain costs by up to 67% relative to the Georgia Tech student teams; conversely, weaker non-reasoning models operating decentralized (e.g., Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o mini) produced costs an order of magnitude higher than humans via bullwhip effects, with run-to-run cost variability of 13%-46% of the mean (Llama 4 Maverick 17B showed the greatest variability), and models such as Phi-4 and DeepSeek-R1-0528 failed to produce a validly formatted decision in over 25% of trials. A simple per-agent budget guardrail cut costs by 25% (GPT-5 mini), 39% (GPT-4o mini), and 41% (Llama 4 Maverick 17B), and reduced Llama 4 Maverick 17B's run-to-run variability from 46% to 37%. Sharing a 5-week demand history via a central orchestrator cut GPT-4o mini's costs by 69% but degraded performance for already-capable reasoning models. Reframing the prompt objective from 'minimize total costs' to 'minimize weighted backlog + holding costs' cut costs 44% (GPT-4o mini) and 33% (GPT-4.1 mini), with negligible effect on more capable models.
core_contribution: This is a 233-page Harvard PhD dissertation (Applied Mathematics, Xuan (Carol) Long, advised by Flavio Calmon) organized around three self-contained pillars, each corresponding to the author's own published or in-progress papers: (1) Reliability of predictive ML models — formalizes 'arbitrariness' (individual-level prediction disagreement among equally-accurate models) as a third evaluation axis beyond accuracy and group fairness, shows group-fairness interventions can paradoxically increase arbitrariness, and proposes an ensemble algorithm that provably reduces it (Ch.2, based on Long, Hsu, Alghamdi & Calmon, NeurIPS 2023); also develops KMAcc, a kernel-based multiaccuracy method formulated via integral probability metrics in an RKHS (Ch.3). (2) Accountability of generative models via LLM watermarking — the only pillar squarely in AI-security scope. Ch.4.1 poses one-shot watermarking as hypothesis testing with side information, derives the minimax-optimal Correlated Channel (CC) watermark under a min-entropy (worst-case token distribution) constraint. Ch.4.2 extends this to low-entropy regimes with two new distortion-free schemes: SimplexWater (binary scores built from Simplex/Plotkin-bound codes, proved minimax-optimal among binary-score watermarks) and HeavyWater (scores drawn from heavy-tailed distributions, which generalizes the existing Gumbel/Aaronson watermark as a special case). (3) Trustworthiness of autonomous multi-agent LLM systems — builds what the thesis calls 'the first' fully LLM-driven simulator of the MIT Beer Distribution Game supply-chain exercise, benchmarking gen-AI agents (GPT-5 mini, GPT-4o mini, Llama 4 Maverick 17B, Llama 3.3 70B, Phi-4, DeepSeek-R1-0528) against >100 Georgia Tech undergraduates across 12 cohorts, and studies how guardrails, curated information-sharing via a central orchestrator, and prompt design affect cost and the bullwhip effect.
threat_model: A threat model is present ONLY for the watermarking pillar (Ch.4), and is not imposed on the other two pillars. The setting: a watermark generator embeds a signal into LLM-generated text via shared side information S between generator and detector; the adversary is either (a) a distribution 'nature' choosing the worst-case (min-entropy-constrained) next-token distribution QX to minimize detectability, motivating the minimax framing of CC/SimplexWater/HeavyWater, or (b) an active adversary attempting to strip or tamper with a watermark in generated text before it is redistributed as ostensibly human-authored content (evaluated empirically as 'robustness to textual attacks' in Appendix C.6.3, not extracted in full here). The stated goal of the watermark is provenance/authenticity attribution of AI-generated text, i.e., distinguishing AI-generated from human-written content and, potentially, attributing origin. The fairness/arbitrariness pillar (Ch.2-3) is a responsible-ML reliability problem (no adversary), and the supply-chain pillar (Ch.5) studies systemic/tail risk in multi-agent coordination, not adversarial manipulation by an attacker.
limitations: The dissertation itself notes (Ch.4.1 conclusion) that the CC one-shot analysis has not yet been extended to positive-perception regimes (allowing minor, non-zero distortion for higher detection) or fully to robustness against adversarial manipulation and embedding capacity, both flagged as future work. The watermark baselines were selected 'based on code availability or ease of implementation' rather than being exhaustive. On HumanEval, the proposed watermarks do not dominate all baselines on all pass@k metrics (Gumbel beats SimplexWater/HeavyWater on pass@1). For the supply-chain pillar, the comparison is against a specific student population (Georgia Tech undergraduates) using a single classic simulation (Beer Distribution Game), and results depend heavily on model choice, prompt design, and guardrails set by the experimenters -- the thesis frames this as an early lab demonstration ('at least in the lab') rather than a production deployment, and explicitly reports catastrophic failure modes for weaker/older models, meaning generalization to other real-world supply chains or newer/other model families is untested here. The fairness/arbitrariness and watermarking chapters are separately-published papers stitched into a single document; cross-chapter synthesis (e.g., whether the same techniques interact) is not attempted.

datasets_used: LCC (code completion prompt dataset, watermarking evaluation)
WaterBench (benchmarking protocol/dataset suite: LongformQA, Multi-news Summarization, Knowledge Memorization, Knowledge Understanding)
MIT Beer Distribution Game simulator (author-built, multi-agent supply-chain testbed)
12 Georgia Tech undergraduate cohorts (>100 students) playing the Beer Game as the human baseline
FolkTables (Income-WA, Health-WI, Employment, Mobility tasks) and ENEM, HSLS, Adult datasets (used in the fairness/arbitrariness chapters, Ch.2-3, outside security scope)
quantitative_results: HumanEval pass@1/pass@5/pass@10 (%): No Watermark 14.0/20.7/28.1; HeavyWater 13.1/18.9/27.8; SimplexWater 13.7/22.7/25.3; Gumbel 14.3/20.5/25.6; Inverse Transform 13.8/22.0/27.5; Red/Green (delta=3) 11.6/19.5/23.2
Up to 250% improvement in aggregated -log10(p) detection strength when substituting Red-Green (delta=2) with SimplexWater/HeavyWater under the same hashing scheme
Beer Game: best AI configuration (Llama 4 Maverick 17B, optimized prompts + orchestrator + guardrails) reduced costs up to 67% vs. human student teams
Decentralized weaker models (e.g., Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o mini) produced costs an order of magnitude higher than human teams; older-generation models produced costs up to 5x higher than humans
Run-to-run cost variability across identical simulation runs: 13%-46% of the mean, with Llama 4 Maverick 17B showing the greatest variability
Phi-4 and DeepSeek-R1-0528 failed to produce validly formatted decisions in over 25% of trials
Budget guardrail reduced total costs by 25% (GPT-5 mini), 39% (GPT-4o mini), 41% (Llama 4 Maverick 17B); Llama 4 Maverick 17B variability fell from 46% to 37%
Sharing 5-week demand history via central orchestrator cut GPT-4o mini costs by 69%
Reframed objective prompt cut costs by 44% (GPT-4o mini) and 33% (GPT-4.1 mini)
baselines_compared: Gumbel/Aaronson watermark (Aaronson 2023) -- prior work, not the author's
SynthID watermark (K=15 competition layers) -- prior work
Correlated Channel (CC) watermark -- author's own Ch.4.1 contribution, reused as a comparator in Ch.4.2 experiments
LSBoost and MCBoost -- prior work, baselines for the (non-security) kernel-multiaccuracy chapter
GPT-5 mini, GPT-4o mini, GPT-4.1 mini, Llama 4 Maverick 17B, Llama 3.3 70B, Phi-4, DeepSeek-R1-0528 -- LLM agents compared in the (non-security) multi-agent supply-chain chapter
